summary: PaymentGatewayClient throws SocketTimeoutException after 30s
description: The PaymentGatewayClient throws a SocketTimeoutException when the payment gateway does not respond within the 30-second hard timeout. The HTTP POST request to the payment API hangs, the connection is never released, and the transaction fails silently. Stack trace: PaymentGatewayClient.processPayment line 247. No retry logic exists. Affects billing module.

summary: Payment API returns HTTP 504 Gateway Timeout intermittently
description: During peak hours the payment processing API returns HTTP 504 Gateway Timeout errors. The PaymentGatewayClient does not implement exponential backoff or retry logic. The billing transaction is marked as failed and requires manual reprocessing. Network logs show the gateway connection timeout at 28–32 seconds.
comments: Gateway team says no issues on their end

summary: Billing module hangs on payment gateway connection timeout — no fallback
description: When the external payment gateway is slow to respond, the billing module hangs indefinitely waiting for the TCP connection. The PaymentGatewayClient has no connection timeout configured, resulting in thread starvation under load. The billing service becomes unresponsive for all users until the gateway recovers.
comments: Fixed by adding 5s connect timeout — but issue returned

summary: PaymentGatewayClient request timeout causes duplicate transaction attempts
description: When a payment request times out at 30 seconds, the system retries the same transaction without idempotency checks. This causes duplicate billing charges for customers. The PaymentGatewayClient sends the same POST request without a unique transaction ID, and the gateway processes it multiple times.

summary: Gateway timeout error not surfaced to user — silent failure in payment flow
description: When the payment gateway times out, the billing module swallows the SocketTimeoutException and returns a generic success response. The customer sees a success message but the payment was never processed. The transaction log shows TIMEOUT_ERROR but the UI shows PAYMENT_CONFIRMED.
comments: Silent failure is the worst behavior

summary: PaymentGateway connection pool exhausted during timeout storm
description: During a gateway timeout event, all connection pool threads are blocked waiting for the payment gateway response. The connection pool (max 10 connections) is exhausted within seconds. Subsequent payment requests are rejected immediately. PaymentGatewayClient.getConnection blocks until timeout propagates.
comments: Pool size increased to 50 as workaround

summary: HTTP 408 Request Timeout from payment gateway in load testing
description: Load testing with 50 concurrent users reveals that payment gateway returns HTTP 408 Request Timeout for 15% of transactions. PaymentGatewayClient does not implement circuit breaker pattern. When timeout rate exceeds 10%, the entire billing service degrades. Retry storm amplifies the problem.

summary: Payment gateway API call timeout not configurable — hardcoded 30s
description: The payment gateway timeout is hardcoded to 30 seconds in PaymentGatewayClient class. Different environments require different timeout values. There is no configuration property or environment variable to override the timeout. Production requires 10s, staging requires 60s for slow test gateway.

summary: Billing payment gateway timeout — no monitoring or alerting configured
description: Payment gateway timeout errors are logged but no alert fires when timeout rate exceeds threshold. Operations team is unaware of timeout storms until customers complain. PaymentGatewayClient logs ERROR level but monitoring dashboard only shows INFO. Need Datadog alert for timeout_rate > 5%.
comments: Monitoring gap identified in incident review

summary: Recurring payment gateway timeout — third occurrence this quarter
description: This is the third occurrence of the payment gateway timeout issue this quarter. Previous fixes (pool size increase, 5s connect timeout) did not resolve the root cause. The payment transaction fails when gateway response time exceeds 30s. PaymentGatewayClient needs a complete redesign with retry, circuit breaker, and configurable timeout.

summary: PaymentGatewayClient fails to release connection after timeout
description: After a gateway timeout, the PaymentGatewayClient does not properly close the HTTP connection. The dangling connection is not returned to the pool. Over time this causes a connection leak. Connections accumulate until the pool is exhausted. Restart required to recover billing service.

summary: Gateway timeout increases billing processing time from 2s to 35s avg
description: Average billing processing time has increased from 2 seconds to 35 seconds due to payment gateway timeouts. Performance monitoring shows P99 latency at 38 seconds. The PaymentGatewayClient waits the full 30-second timeout before failing. This degrades the end-to-end billing workflow significantly.

summary: Payment gateway timeout — carrier XYZ reports billing failures
description: Insurance carrier XYZ reports that 12 billing transactions failed on 2025-08-15 due to payment gateway timeouts. PaymentGatewayClient logs show TIMEOUT_ERROR at exactly 30000ms. Customer-visible error: 'Payment processing failed. Please try again.' Carrier requests RCA and resolution timeline.

summary: PaymentGatewayClient timeout — no graceful degradation to offline queue
description: When payment gateway times out, there is no fallback to an offline queue for later retry. The transaction is simply dropped. A message queue approach would allow the system to queue failed payment requests and retry them when the gateway recovers. PaymentGatewayClient should integrate with the existing message broker.

summary: Premium calculation returns incorrect rate for multi-location policy
description: The LossControlPremiumCalc module returns an incorrect premium rate for policies covering multiple locations. The rate calculation algorithm does not properly aggregate location-specific risk factors. For a 3-location policy, the premium is calculated as if all locations have the same risk profile as location 1. Expected premium: $42,500. Actual premium calculated: $38,200.

summary: Rounding error in premium calculation — decimal precision lost
description: The premium calculation formula in LossControlPremiumCalc performs intermediate division operations using integer arithmetic, causing decimal precision loss. A policy with base rate 0.00234 is rounded to 0.002, introducing a $450 error per policy. The rounding issue compounds across endorsements.
comments: Reopened — edge case still incorrect for umbrella policies

summary: Policy premium endorsement override not applied in calculation
description: When an underwriter applies a manual endorsement override to a policy premium, the LossControlPremiumCalc module ignores the override and recalculates from base rates. The endorsement value is stored in the database but not read during the calculation step. PolicyPremiumService.calculate() does not check for active endorsements.

summary: Incorrect premium rate factor applied for commercial auto class
description: The premium rate calculation for commercial auto insurance class applies the wrong rate factor table. LossControlPremiumCalc loads rate factors from the RATE_FACTOR_TABLE but uses the personal auto factors (table ID 101) instead of commercial auto (table ID 205). All commercial auto policies are underpriced by approximately 18%.

summary: Premium calculation algorithm produces negative rate for low-risk policies
description: For policies with very low risk scores (below 15), the premium calculation algorithm produces a negative premium value. The LossControlPremiumCalc.applyRiskDiscount() method subtracts a discount that exceeds the base premium. Negative premiums are stored in the database and displayed to users without validation.
comments: Edge case for ultra-low risk resolved

summary: Policy renewal premium calculation ignores prior loss history
description: When calculating renewal premiums, the LossControlPremiumCalc module does not factor in the policy's prior loss history. The experience modification factor (EMF) is not retrieved from the claims database during renewal calculation. Renewing policies with high loss history receive the same premium as new clean policies.

summary: Premium calculation mismatch between quote and bind — $1,200 difference
description: A customer reports a $1,200 difference between the quoted premium and the bound policy premium. Investigation shows LossControlPremiumCalc uses different rate tables at quote time vs bind time. The rate table is refreshed nightly and changed between the quote (dated yesterday) and bind (today).
comments: Quote rate should be locked at issue time

summary: Incorrect tax and surcharge calculation in final premium
description: The final premium calculation in LossControlPremiumCalc applies state tax and surcharges in the wrong order. Tax is calculated before surcharges are added, but state regulations require surcharges to be applied first, then tax. This results in an incorrect total premium for policies in states TX, FL, and CA.

summary: Premium recalculation on mid-term endorsement generates wrong pro-rata amount
description: When a mid-term endorsement is added, LossControlPremiumCalc calculates the pro-rata premium adjustment incorrectly. It uses 365 days as the policy year divisor but the actual policy term is 364 days (leap year adjustment missing). This causes a small but systematic error on all mid-term endorsement premiums.

summary: Rate algorithm produces wrong premium for umbrella policy layering
description: Umbrella policies with underlying coverage layers have their premium calculated incorrectly. LossControlPremiumCalc does not account for the attachment point when calculating umbrella rates. The algorithm treats umbrella as a standalone policy rather than excess coverage, resulting in overpriced umbrella premiums by 22%.

summary: Premium calculation performance: 8 seconds for complex multi-line policy
description: Complex multi-line policies with 5+ coverages take 8 seconds for premium calculation due to N+1 database queries in LossControlPremiumCalc. Each coverage triggers a separate rate table lookup query. Batching rate table lookups would reduce calculation time to under 1 second.

summary: Wrong premium formula applied after LossControlPremiumCalc v2 migration
description: After the migration to LossControlPremiumCalc v2, some policy types (specifically BOP — Business Owners Policy) continue to use the v1 formula due to an incorrect feature flag configuration. BOP policies show premium values 12% lower than expected. The v2 formula includes additional risk factors that v1 did not.

summary: Risk assessment form submits without required field validation
description: The risk assessment form in the Loss Control module allows submission without completing required fields. The 'Occupancy Type' and 'Construction Class' fields are marked required in the UI but server-side validation does not enforce them. Incomplete assessments are saved to the database with null values, causing downstream calculation failures.

summary: Assessment form validation error message not displayed on blank mandatory fields
description: When a user submits the risk assessment form with blank mandatory fields, no error message is displayed. The form silently clears and re-renders without highlighting the invalid fields. The validation logic runs but the error state is not propagated to the form component. Fields: Sprinkler System, Roof Age, Building Value.
comments: Reopened — error messages missing on edge case multi-tab form

summary: Risk survey form accepts invalid numeric input — negative building value
description: The risk assessment form accepts negative values for the 'Building Replacement Value' field. Input validation only checks that the field is not blank but does not validate the range. A user can enter -500000 as the building value and the system accepts it, resulting in negative insured values and incorrect premium calculations.
comments: Linked to LC-2005 — negative premium issue

summary: Mandatory risk assessment field check bypassed via API direct call
description: Server-side mandatory field validation in the risk assessment API can be bypassed by calling the REST endpoint directly. The validation is only implemented in the form UI JavaScript, not in the backend RiskAssessmentService.save() method. API consumers and integrations can create assessments with missing required fields.

summary: Risk assessment form field validation inconsistent between create and edit modes
description: When creating a new risk assessment, 12 fields are validated as required. When editing an existing assessment, only 5 of those fields are validated. The edit mode form has a different validation schema than the create mode. Users can remove required field values during edit and save incomplete assessments.

summary: Form validation does not prevent submit on network error — data loss
description: When the risk assessment form submit fails due to a network error, the form validation state is reset and the user's input is lost. The user must re-enter all form data. Form validation should preserve field state on submission error and display an appropriate error message without clearing the form.
comments: Form state not preserved on error

summary: Conditional required fields in risk assessment form not validated correctly
description: The risk assessment form has conditional required fields — some fields become required only when other fields have specific values. The validation logic checks all fields as always-required or always-optional, ignoring conditional rules. For example, 'Alarm System Type' should be required when 'Security System: Yes' but is not validated.
comments: Conditional logic not implemented in validator

summary: Risk assessment form submit button stays enabled during validation
description: The Submit button on the risk assessment form remains enabled while client-side validation is running. Users can click Submit multiple times during validation, resulting in duplicate form submissions. Server receives 2-3 identical assessment save requests. Duplicate records created in the database.

summary: Risk assessment form date field validation allows future inspection dates
description: The 'Last Inspection Date' field on the risk assessment form accepts future dates. Validation only checks the date format (MM/DD/YYYY) but not the date range. Inspections cannot occur in the future, so the field should reject any date after today. This causes data quality issues in the risk database.
comments: Low priority but data quality impact

summary: Assessment form validation error on special characters in address field
description: The risk assessment form throws a validation error when the insured address contains special characters such as # (unit number) or - (hyphenated street names). The address field regex validation pattern rejects valid addresses like '123 Oak-Street #4B'. The validation pattern is too restrictive.

summary: Loss Control report generation takes 45 seconds for large policy portfolios
description: Generating the Loss Control quarterly summary report for portfolios with over 500 policies takes 45 seconds. The report generator executes a database query that fetches all policy records with N+1 sub-queries for each policy's claims history. The report PDF generation also loads all data into memory simultaneously.

summary: Report export to PDF times out for reports with more than 200 pages
description: The PDF export function in the report module times out when generating reports exceeding 200 pages. The report generation server has a 60-second timeout. Large reports require 75-90 seconds to generate and export. The PDF library (Apache PDFBox) loads all page content into memory before writing, causing OOM on large reports.

summary: Scheduled report generation causes database performance degradation
description: The nightly scheduled report generation job runs at 2 AM and executes complex aggregation queries that lock report tables for 15 minutes. During this window, other database operations (policy updates, premium calculations) experience significant slowdown. The report query does not use read replicas.

summary: Report loading spinner displayed indefinitely — no timeout handling
description: When a report takes longer than 30 seconds to generate, the loading spinner continues indefinitely. There is no timeout handler or user feedback for long-running report generation. Users wait over a minute without knowing if the report will complete or has failed silently. No cancel mechanism exists.
comments: Async report generation with status polling needed

summary: Large data export causes memory spike and server restart
description: Exporting a large Loss Control data report (50,000+ records) to Excel causes the application server memory to spike from 2GB to 14GB, triggering an OOM kill and server restart. The data export streams all records into a single in-memory list before writing to the Excel file. Streaming write needed.

summary: Report generation query missing index — full table scan on 2M records
description: The Loss Control activity report generation query performs a full table scan on the POLICY_ACTIVITY table (2 million records) due to a missing database index on the CREATED_DATE column. Adding an index reduces query time from 40 seconds to 800ms. The query plan shows Seq Scan instead of Index Scan.

summary: Concurrent report generation requests overload report server
description: When 5 or more users generate reports simultaneously, the report server becomes unresponsive. Each report generation spawns a dedicated thread and all threads compete for database connections. The report server has no request queuing or rate limiting. Connection pool of 20 is exhausted by concurrent report jobs.
comments: Report queue with max concurrent jobs needed

summary: Report generation slow due to unoptimized JOIN on claims and policy tables
description: The claims summary report generator uses a slow multi-table JOIN across the POLICY, CLAIMS, COVERAGE, and PREMIUM tables without proper indexing. The query execution plan shows a nested loop join with 1.2 million row scans. Re-writing the query with hash joins and adding a composite index reduces time from 52s to 4s.

summary: PDF report generation duplicates header on every page
description: The generated PDF report incorrectly renders the report header section on every page instead of only the first page. The PDF template configuration has the header defined as a repeating section. This increases PDF file size by 40% and makes reports look unprofessional. Affects all report types.

summary: User session expires mid-workflow — unsaved risk assessment data lost
description: User sessions expire after 30 minutes of idle time. When a user is completing a long risk assessment form and the session expires mid-workflow, the form data is lost upon submission. The authentication redirect clears the form state. Users lose up to 20 minutes of entered data. Session expiry warning not shown.
comments: Session should extend on user activity

summary: JWT token refresh fails silently — user sees blank screen
description: When the JWT authentication token expires and the auto-refresh attempt fails (due to network error), the application renders a blank screen instead of redirecting to the login page. The token refresh endpoint returns 401 but the frontend fetch handler does not redirect on auth failure. Users are stuck on a blank screen.
comments: Reopened — blank screen still seen on mobile browsers

summary: Session cookie not invalidated on logout — reuse possible
description: After a user logs out, the session cookie remains valid on the server for the full 24-hour TTL. A malicious actor with access to the cookie can reuse it to authenticate as the logged-out user. The logout endpoint only clears the cookie on the client but does not invalidate the session token in the server-side session store.

summary: Idle session timeout too aggressive — 30 minutes not enough for risk surveys
description: The 30-minute idle session timeout is too short for users completing comprehensive risk surveys that can take 45-60 minutes. After session expiry, the user is redirected to login and loses all survey progress. The timeout should be configurable per user role, with risk assessors getting a 90-minute idle timeout.

summary: Multiple concurrent login sessions allowed — audit compliance issue
description: The authentication system allows the same user to have unlimited concurrent active sessions from different devices. Audit compliance requires that users have at most 2 concurrent sessions. When a third session is created, the oldest session should be automatically invalidated. Session tracking across devices not implemented.

summary: Auth token not refreshed when user returns from background tab
description: When a browser tab with the Loss Control application is in the background for more than 30 minutes, the JWT token expires. When the user returns to the tab, the token is not automatically refreshed. API calls fail with 401 and the user must manually reload the page and log in again. Token refresh should trigger on tab focus.
comments: Tab visibility API should trigger refresh

summary: Session expiry during report download causes partial file download
description: If the user's session expires mid-download during a large report export, the file download is truncated. The browser receives a 302 redirect to the login page mid-stream, resulting in a corrupt partially-downloaded report file. Report download should use a pre-signed download token independent of session state.

summary: Login redirect loop after session expiry on SSO-enabled accounts
description: Users authenticated via SSO (Single Sign-On) get stuck in a redirect loop when their session expires. The application redirects to the SSO IdP, which immediately redirects back to the application, which again detects an expired session and redirects to IdP. The loop continues indefinitely. Affects all Azure AD SSO users.
comments: SSO redirect loop affects all Azure AD users

summary: CSV data import fails on files with UTF-8 BOM encoding
description: The Loss Control data import module fails to parse CSV files that have a UTF-8 BOM (Byte Order Mark) at the start of the file. The CSV parser reads the BOM as part of the first column header, resulting in a column name mismatch error. Files exported from Excel have UTF-8 BOM by default. Import fails with: 'Unknown column: \uFEFFPolicy_ID'.

summary: Batch CSV import silently skips rows with date format mismatch
description: When importing policy data via CSV batch import, rows with dates in MM/DD/YYYY format are silently skipped if the expected format is YYYY-MM-DD. The import completes successfully with a row count that does not match the source file. No error log or warning is generated. Users are unaware of the data loss.
comments: Warning log added for skipped rows

summary: CSV import column header mapping is case-sensitive — breaks on uppercase headers
description: The CSV import column header mapping is case-sensitive. Files with headers in uppercase (POLICY_ID, PREMIUM_AMOUNT) fail to map to the expected lowercase headers (policy_id, premium_amount). The import rejects the file with a 'Required column missing' error. Header mapping should be case-insensitive.
comments: Different source systems use different cases

summary: Large CSV file import (50MB) fails with out-of-memory error
description: Importing large CSV files (50MB, 200,000 rows) causes an out-of-memory error. The import service reads the entire file into memory as a string before parsing. For a 50MB file this causes a 500MB memory spike (due to Java string overhead). Streaming CSV parsing row-by-row would prevent the OOM error.
comments: 50MB file is common for annual batch imports

summary: CSV import does not handle quoted fields with embedded commas
description: The CSV import parser does not correctly handle quoted fields that contain embedded commas. A policy address like '"123 Main St, Suite 400"' is split incorrectly into two fields: '"123 Main St' and 'Suite 400"'. The parser uses a simple comma-split instead of a proper RFC 4180 compliant CSV parser.

summary: CSV batch import row validation errors not reported per-row
description: When a batch CSV import file contains rows with validation errors (missing required fields, invalid values), the import fails entirely without identifying which specific rows have errors. The user must manually check the entire file. Validation should report errors per-row with line numbers so users can fix only the problematic rows.

summary: CSV import delimiter auto-detection fails for semicolon-delimited files
description: European carriers export CSV files using semicolons as delimiters instead of commas. The Loss Control import module does not auto-detect the delimiter and assumes comma-separated format. Semicolon-delimited files fail to parse correctly — all data is imported into the first column. Users must manually convert files before import.
